stories.headline: Greeley voters repeal approval of $1.1 billion Cascadia development project
stories.summary:
Greeley residents voted Tuesday to repeal the city ordinance approving the Cascadia development, a massive $1.1 billion mixed-use project that would have included an 8,600-seat arena for the Colorado Eagles, a conference hotel, an indoor waterpark and housing across 800 acres in West Greeley. The unofficial vote count was 11,342 to 9,506 in favor of repeal, with official results expected March 5.

The citizens group Greeley Demands Better gathered more than 4,586 signatures to trigger the special election after the Greeley City Council approved the project 5-2. Opponents argued the project posed severe financial risk to taxpayers, with the city planning to finance it through bonding, general improvement district financing and projected operating revenues.

Co-chair Brandon Wark called the result a historic David vs. Goliath victory, noting opponents were significantly outspent. With the ordinance repealed, the land reverts to agricultural zoning, according to Greeley public information officer Kim Kappel.

Primary developer Martin Lind of Water Valley sued the City of Greeley on Monday in an attempt to void the special election. That legal challenge was pending as results were announced.

stories.headline: Aaron Gordon progressing in hamstring recovery, Nuggets coach says return is near
stories.summary:
Denver Nuggets forward Aaron Gordon has resumed nearly full practice participation, head coach David Adelman said, describing him as doing "basically everything but the contact stuff" in a recent session that included situational defensive drills. Gordon has missed 35 games this season due to two separate hamstring strains, the most games lost to injury in a single season since he joined the team at the 2021 trade deadline.

Adelman said the next threshold for Gordon's return is participating in contact drills, adding that getting through those sessions would bring confidence to the entire organization. The coach acknowledged the frustration Gordon has felt watching the team win and lose without him during a critical stretch of the season.

In the 23 games Gordon has played this season, he has averaged 17.5 points, 6.2 rebounds and 2.5 assists while shooting 50.9 percent from the field and 40 percent from three-point range, both career-best marks in Denver. His absence has been a significant factor in the Nuggets' recent struggles in the Western Conference standings.

No timetable for Gordon's return has been set, but Adelman indicated the team intends to have him healthy for the final stretch of the regular season ahead of the playoffs.

stories.headline: Colorado deploys first interstate speed cameras on I-25 north of Denver starting March 1
stories.summary:
Colorado is activating eight automated speed enforcement cameras along Interstate 25 between Mead and Berthoud starting March 1, marking the first deployment of speed cameras on a Colorado interstate. The cameras are placed within an active construction work zone where crews are building new express lanes, and drivers will receive one-time warning notices for approximately one month before civil fines of $75 take effect around April 2.

The Colorado Department of Transportation said tests conducted over the past month found roughly 10 percent of drivers exceeded the 65 mph speed limit by more than 10 mph. Signs will be posted at least 300 feet ahead of each camera, and violations will not result in driver's license point deductions. Revenue generated will primarily fund the enforcement program, with excess dollars directed toward protecting vulnerable road users.

The I-25 deployment follows the introduction of speed cameras last July along Colorado 119 between Boulder and Longmont, where CDOT issued 34,000 warnings between July and December 2025. Officials reported an 80 percent reduction in high-speed driving in that work zone since the warning period began, and 5,405 violations have been issued since full enforcement started in January.

CDOT spokesman Matt Inzeo said the agency may expand speed camera use to additional interstates, though no timetable has been established. Colorado lawmakers authorized automated speed enforcement in 2023. Speed was a contributing factor in more than 1,200 crashes and 91 fatalities on Colorado roads in 2025.

stories.headline: High winds trigger DIA ground stop, hundreds of flight delays and cancellations
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport experienced a ground stop Tuesday afternoon due to high winds sweeping across the metro area, with federal officials warning additional stops or delays were possible into the evening. As of 5:35 p.m., FlightAware data showed 386 flights delayed and 25 canceled at DIA.

SkyWest reported 119 delays, United Airlines had 100 delays and 12 cancellations, and Southwest Airlines logged 108 delays and six cancellations. National Weather Service sensors recorded sustained winds as high as 37 mph with gusts reaching 47 mph Tuesday afternoon.

Forecasters issued a red flag warning for the Front Range and Eastern Plains due to critical fire weather conditions, including the high winds combined with low humidity. The DIA ground stop was lifted Tuesday afternoon, though the FAA cautioned disruptions could continue.

stories.headline: DIA reverses course, will evaluate mass transit expansion as alternative to widening Peña Boulevard
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport officials have expanded the scope of their plan to address worsening traffic congestion on Peña Boulevard, agreeing to evaluate mass transit improvements alongside the previously planned roadway widening. The shift came last month as a required National Environmental Policy Act review began for the project, which is expected to cost hundreds of millions of dollars with funding still uncertain.

Under the revised approach, boosting RTD A Line train frequency by adding track and constructing bus-only lanes will be evaluated as core alternatives. DIA project manager Danielle Yearsley confirmed that "rail expansion and other transit alternatives are under consideration" and that options previously screened out by the Peña Master Plan are now on the table.

Environmental and transit advocacy groups including GreenLatinos and Greater Denver Transit had challenged the highway widening plan, arguing road expansions attract more traffic over time and worsen air pollution. The average drive time on Peña Boulevard from I-70 to DIA's terminal has tripled from eight minutes to 24 minutes, with peak-period jams sometimes exceeding 30 minutes.

The RTD A Line currently runs every 15 minutes during peak travel at a cost of $10 one-way, but service drops to roughly every 30 minutes in evenings and early mornings. Bus service to and from DIA often runs only once per hour. The Federal Aviation Administration must ultimately approve DIA's final decision following the NEPA review.

stories.headline: Two Tren de Aragua members sentenced to 20 years in federal prison for Denver jewelry store robbery
stories.summary:
Two members of the Venezuelan gang Tren de Aragua were sentenced to 20 years each in federal prison for the June 2024 armed robbery of Joyeria El Ruby at 5108 W. 38th Ave. in Denver's West Highland neighborhood, the U.S. Attorney's Office for the District of Colorado announced. Jean Torres-Ramon, 22, received a sentence of 235 months and Newman Castillo Delgado, 23, received an even 20 years after both pleaded guilty to robbery and brandishing a firearm in furtherance of a crime of violence.

The two men and at least five co-defendants are accused of entering the store, pointing guns at employees, beating workers with weapons and stealing nearly $4 million in gold and jewelry. Both Torres-Ramon and Castillo Delgado were ordered to pay $3.9 million in restitution. A separate federal indictment alleges the robbery was authorized by Tren de Aragua leadership to enrich the gang.

Five additional suspects in the case — Oswaldo Lozada-Solis, Jesus Daniel Lara Del Toro, Derek Alexander Dun-Vargas, Briley Ballera-Farias and Edwuimar Nazareth Colina-Romero — still have open federal cases, according to court records.

stories.headline: Denver AI firm Rank One Computing goes public on Nasdaq, raising $24 million
stories.summary:
Denver-based artificial intelligence company Rank One Computing, known as ROC, made its public market debut Friday on the Nasdaq Capital Market under the ticker symbol ROC, pricing shares at $6 and raising $24 million in its initial public offering. Strong demand triggered an overallotment option allowing the underwriter to purchase an additional 600,000 shares within 30 days. Shares closed Tuesday at $5.80, giving the company a market valuation of approximately $95 million.

ROC, founded in 2015, specializes in image recognition technology including facial recognition, fingerprint reading, iris scanning, tattoo identification, license plate detection and weapon identification. The company's Vision AI platform is marketed as less hardware-intensive than competitors, capable of operating without expensive graphics processing units. In one benchmark test, ROC said its system read fingerprints 500 times faster than rival vendors.

The company has 42 employees and counts U.S. federal agencies, state and local law enforcement, global financial firms and commercial security organizations among its dozens of paying customers. Its headquarters is located at 1290 Broadway in Denver, with additional offices in Grand Rapids, Michigan and Morgantown, West Virginia.

ROC's IPO comes one week after Palantir Technologies abruptly announced its relocation from Denver to Miami, and represents the second Colorado company to go public in 2026. York Space Systems, a Greenwood Village satellite manufacturer, raised $629 million in a January IPO and carries a market value of approximately $3.54 billion.

stories.headline: National mock drafts project Broncos to target linebacker or receiver with 30th overall pick
stories.summary:
With the NFL Combine underway in Indianapolis, national draft analysts are converging on linebacker and wide receiver as the most likely directions for the Denver Broncos at No. 30 overall in the 2026 NFL Draft. Multiple outlets, including NFL.com, The Athletic and USA Today, project Denver to select Georgia linebacker Damon Allen, citing his sideline-to-sideline speed, tackling production and fit within defensive coordinator Vance Joseph's scheme.

Allen led Georgia with 88 tackles in 2025 and was effective in pass coverage despite lacking the size of recent high-profile Georgia linebacker prospects. The Athletic's Chris Kamrani noted that Denver has consistently hit on defensive draft picks in recent years and that Allen could serve as the signal-caller of a defense built around a dominant front and elite secondary.

CBS Sports diverged from the consensus, projecting the Broncos to select a Big Ten wide receiver to add another weapon for quarterback Bo Nix. ESPN's Mel Kiper Jr. projected a small-school safety as a potential sleeper pick for Denver. The Broncos hold the 30th overall selection after finishing the 2025 season with one of the NFL's top defenses.

stories.headline: Colorado school funding fight intensifies as Polis vehicle tax proposal threatens district budgets
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis is proposing to restructure how the state counts proceeds from the specific ownership tax — an annual levy on vehicle registrations — in a move that education advocates say would effectively cut tens of millions of dollars from school district budgets. Under current law, additional specific ownership tax dollars generated when districts pass bonds or mill levy overrides are kept locally and remain outside the state's school funding formula. Polis wants to fold all of those dollars into the formula and redistribute them statewide.

The change would produce two compounding effects for school districts: it would increase the local share of school funding, allowing the state to reduce its own contribution, and it would redistribute dollars away from districts that passed local tax measures toward districts that did not. Denver Public Schools chief finance officer Chuck Carpenter said the proposal amounts to an unambiguous cut to K-12 education funding that districts are legally owed.

During the current school year, specific ownership tax funds sent to all Colorado districts totaled an estimated $451.8 million, of which approximately $190.5 million was not counted in districts' local share and remained under local control. Polis' office has framed the change as necessary to sustain the state's new school funding formula amid chronic budget shortfalls. Education advocates and the Colorado School Finance Project have strongly opposed the characterization that these funds are "sheltered," arguing districts receive and spend the money lawfully.
